h1Title: Acute Dacryocystitis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Acute dacryocystitis is a bacterial infection of the lacrimal sac — the tear-collecting reservoir at the medial corner of the eye — caused by obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct that allows stagnant tears to become colonized by bacteria. It accounts for the majority of acute periorbital infections presenting to ophthalmology emergency services and most commonly affects adults over 40 years and neonates within the first weeks of life. The condition presents with sudden erythema, induration, and exquisite tenderness over the medial canthus below the medial palpebral ligament, accompanied by epiphora and mucopurulent discharge. Although most cases resolve with systemic antibiotics and warm compresses, approximately 10-20% progress to lacrimal sac abscess requiring incision and drainage. Definitive cure requires dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) to bypass the blocked duct.

keyStats.prevalence: Dacryocystitis accounts for approximately 3% of ophthalmology emergency visits; population incidence estimated at 1-3 per 10,000 adults per year in retrospective registry studies
keyStats.demographics: Bimodal distribution: congenital/neonatal peak (first 3 months of life due to imperforate nasolacrimal duct membrane) and adult peak in women over 40 (women 3× more likely due to narrower bony nasolacrimal canal)
keyStats.avgAge: Adult acute dacryocystitis peaks at ages 40-70 years; women represent 55-75% of adult cases in most series
keyStats.globalCases: Global incidence data not reliably reported; congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction (the predisposing condition) affects 2-6% of all newborns

definition: Acute dacryocystitis (ICD-10: H04.32) is an acute bacterial infection of the lacrimal sac, the membranous sac lying in the lacrimal fossa of the medial orbital wall, caused by upstream obstruction of the nasolacrimal duct. The nasolacrimal system drains tears from the medial angle of each eye through the upper and lower lacrimal puncta and canaliculi into the lacrimal sac, then through the nasolacrimal duct into the inferior meatus of the nose. When the duct is obstructed — most often by acquired primary nasolacrimal duct stenosis in middle-aged women, but also by trauma, polyps, stones (dacryoliths), and sinonasal disease — tears stagnate and become colonized by bacteria from the conjunctiva and nasal cavity. The infection is almost always unilateral. Common bacterial pathogens include Staphylococcus aureus (including MRSA), Streptococcus pneumoniae, Haemophilus influenzae, and, in chronic cases, gram-negative bacteria. Acute dacryocystitis presents below the medial palpebral ligament — distinguishing it from orbital cellulitis — and typically manifests as a tender, fluctuant swelling that may progress to frank abscess. Ophthalmologists and oculoplastic surgeons manage both the acute infection and the definitive surgical correction.

typesClassification.description: Most common adult form. Results from primary acquired nasolacrimal duct obstruction from involutional stenosis, chronic inflammation, or dacryoliths. Sudden onset of medial canthal swelling, tenderness, and fever. Treated with systemic antibiotics; DCR surgery for definitive cure once infection resolves.

Caused by failure of the distal nasolacrimal duct membrane (Hasner's valve) to open spontaneously at birth. Presents in the first weeks of life with unilateral epiphora, mucopurulent discharge, and medial canthal swelling. Most congenital duct obstructions resolve spontaneously by 12 months; persistent cases require probing.

Recurrent low-grade infection of the lacrimal sac presenting with persistent epiphora, mucoid regurgitation on pressure over the sac, and chronic conjunctivitis. Often no acute swelling or pain. Predisposes to acute exacerbations. Always requires DCR for permanent cure.

Progression of acute dacryocystitis to frank abscess formation with fluctuant swelling, pointing skin, and systemic sepsis signs. Requires incision and drainage in addition to antibiotics before definitive DCR.

HIV-positive, diabetic, and immunosuppressed patients may present with atypical organisms (fungi, mycobacteria, Nocardia) and more severe infection with orbital spread. Requires broader culture-directed antibiotic coverage.

primarySymptoms: Sudden onset of pain, redness, and swelling over the medial corner of the eye, specifically below the medial palpebral ligament (the inner corner fold), distinguishing dacryocystitis from other causes of periorbital swelling.
Epiphora — persistent watering of the eye — caused by nasolacrimal duct obstruction blocking normal tear drainage.
Mucopurulent or purulent discharge from the medial canthus, often expressible with gentle pressure over the lacrimal sac region.
Tenderness on palpation below and medial to the medial canthus over the lacrimal fossa, frequently exquisite at the peak of infection.
A firm or fluctuant lump at the medial canthus that may have an erythematous, inflamed overlying skin; a fluctuant lump indicates abscess formation.
Fever (temperature above 38°C) and systemic malaise in moderate-to-severe cases, particularly when abscess has formed.
Conjunctival injection and discharge in the affected eye, often as a secondary conjunctivitis from reflux of infected lacrimal sac contents.
History of chronic unilateral watering (epiphora) preceding the acute attack, pointing to underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction.
Localized facial erythema extending toward the nose or cheek in severe cases with spreading preseptal cellulitis.
earlyWarningSigns: Increasing unilateral epiphora (watering eye) that fails to resolve, suggesting developing nasolacrimal duct obstruction — the precursor to dacryocystitis.
Mucoid or mucopurulent discharge expressible on digital pressure over the medial canthus without yet forming an acute painful swelling.
Mild medial canthal discomfort and subtle soft tissue swelling on waking that disappears during the day — suggests intermittent partial obstruction with early sac dilatation.
Recurrent episodes of mild conjunctivitis in one eye without an obvious external cause, particularly in a middle-aged woman — may indicate chronic dacryocystitis with reflux.
emergencySigns: Proptosis (forward displacement of the eyeball), restricted eye movements, or chemosis (swollen conjunctiva) with dacryocystitis — indicates orbital cellulitis with abscess extending into the orbit, requiring urgent CT and IV antibiotics or surgical drainage.
Reduced vision or afferent pupillary defect in an eye with periorbital swelling — suggests orbital apex syndrome or optic nerve compression from spreading infection.
High fever above 39°C with rigors and confusion in a patient with dacryocystitis — sepsis requiring intravenous antibiotics and hospital admission.
Skin necrosis or blackening over the medial canthus — rarely indicates necrotizing fasciitis or mucormycosis in immunocompromised patients; ophthalmologic and infectious disease emergency.

causes.cause: Primary acquired nasolacrimal duct obstruction (involutional stenosis)
causes.description: The most common adult cause: age-related fibrosis and narrowing of the bony nasolacrimal canal, particularly in women whose canal is anatomically narrower than in men. Stagnant tears in the sac become infected by conjunctival and nasal flora. Estimated to underlie over 70% of adult dacryocystitis cases.

causes.cause: Congenital imperforate nasolacrimal duct membrane (Hasner's valve)
causes.description: In neonates, failure of the distal membrane to canalize at birth causes tear stagnation from the outset. Affects 2-6% of newborns; the great majority open spontaneously within 12 months. Secondary infection produces neonatal dacryocystitis within weeks of life.

Midfacial fractures involving the nasolacrimal canal (Le Fort II, orbital floor, naso-orbital-ethmoid fractures), rhinoplasty, endoscopic sinus surgery, and dacryolith formation can all obstruct the lacrimal drainage system and predispose to acute infection.

Inferior turbinate hypertrophy, nasal polyps, and nasal or sinus tumors compress the nasolacrimal duct at its distal opening into the inferior meatus. These causes are identified by nasal endoscopy and explain roughly 10% of acquired duct obstruction.

Calcium, mucous, and epithelial debris can form concretions within the lacrimal sac that partially obstruct outflow and harbor bacteria. Dacryoliths are identified at surgery or occasionally on CT scanning and require removal at DCR.

riskFactors.description: Women account for 55-75% of adult dacryocystitis cases, attributed to the anatomically narrower bony nasolacrimal canal in women. Postmenopausal hormonal changes may contribute to mucosal atrophy in the duct.

The fundamental anatomical precondition for dacryocystitis; stagnant tears in an obstructed sac are the substrate for bacterial colonization. Pre-existing epiphora is present in over 80% of adult patients before the acute episode.
Chronic rhinosinusitis and nasal polyposis are associated with secondary nasolacrimal duct stenosis. Treatment of the nasal disease reduces recurrence risk.
Impaired immune response in diabetes increases severity of dacryocystitis, promotes abscess formation, and raises the risk of atypical organisms. Optimal glucose control reduces infection severity.

riskFactors.factor: Prior facial trauma or sinus surgery
riskFactors.description: Nasolacrimal duct injury from midfacial fractures or endoscopic sinus surgery (particularly anterior ethmoidectomy) creates scar stenosis and infection risk. Post-traumatic DCR may be required.
Immunocompromised patients are at risk of more severe infection, abscess, and orbital spread. Atypical pathogens including fungi and mycobacteria should be considered.

riskFactors.factor: Congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction persisting beyond 12 months
riskFactors.description: In infants with persistent congenital duct obstruction beyond 12-13 months, the risk of recurrent dacryocystitis increases substantially and probing or intubation is indicated.

affectedDemographics: Adult women over 40 account for the majority of cases in most ophthalmology series due to anatomically narrower nasolacrimal canals.
Neonates and infants under 3 months represent the second peak age group, affected by congenital imperforate Hasner's valve.
Patients with chronic sinonasal disease or prior facial surgery are at elevated risk at any age.
Immunocompromised populations — including diabetics, HIV-positive, and those on chemotherapy — have higher rates of severe, complicated, or atypical dacryocystitis.
diagnosisOverview: Acute dacryocystitis is primarily a clinical diagnosis made at the bedside. The hallmark is acute tender swelling below the medial palpebral ligament — the horizontal tendinous band at the medial canthus. This location distinguishes dacryocystitis from periorbital preseptal cellulitis (which is more diffuse), an infected epidermoid cyst of the medial canthus (no epiphora), and angular blepharitis (skin disease rather than deep swelling). Gentle digital pressure over the lacrimal sac often expresses mucopurulent material through the punctum, confirming sac involvement and providing material for culture. The conjunctival swab or expressed discharge should be sent for Gram stain and culture before antibiotics are started, as MRSA and unusual organisms are increasingly seen. If fluctuance suggests abscess formation, or if orbital spread is suspected, contrast CT of the orbits and sinuses is essential to exclude orbital abscess and to plan surgery. CT also images the bony nasolacrimal canal, identifies dacryoliths, and reveals sinonasal disease. Blood count and CRP confirm systemic inflammatory response. Lacrimal system irrigation and probing are contraindicated in the acute phase because of pain and infection risk; these are deferred until after inflammation resolves. MRI is rarely needed unless intracranial extension or tumour is suspected.

diagnosticTests.test: Clinical examination — palpation of lacrimal sac region
diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms the location of swelling as below the medial palpebral ligament (lacrimal fossa) and distinguishes dacryocystitis from preseptal cellulitis, cyst, or sebaceous gland abscess
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: The ophthalmologist places a finger below the medial canthus and gently compresses upward. Expression of pus or mucus through the punctum confirms lacrimal sac involvement. Takes seconds; moderately tender.

diagnosticTests.test: Microbiological culture and sensitivity of expressed discharge
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative organism and antibiotic sensitivities, especially important for MRSA detection and treatment failure
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A swab or expressed pus sample is sent to the microbiology lab. Results available in 24-48 hours. Guides antibiotic adjustment.

diagnosticTests.test: Contrast CT of orbits and sinuses
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates extent of infection, identifies abscess, assesses bony nasolacrimal canal, images sinuses, and excludes orbital spread
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: CT scanner takes 5-10 minutes; contrast dye injected via a cannula. Required in all cases with orbital signs, suspected abscess, treatment failure, or atypical presentation.

diagnosticTests.test: Full blood count and C-reactive protein
diagnosticTests.purpose: Quantifies systemic inflammatory response; leukocytosis with neutrophilia supports acute bacterial infection; elevated CRP guides severity and monitoring
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results within 1-2 hours. Used to guide inpatient versus outpatient management.

diagnosticTests.test: Lacrimal sac irrigation and probing (deferred to resolution phase)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses patency of the lacrimal drainage system to confirm obstruction and plan surgery; performed only once acute infection is fully resolved to avoid sac rupture
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Local anaesthetic drops in the eye; a fine metal probe or cannula is passed through the lower punctum. Saline injected; if it does not reach the nose the obstruction is confirmed. Takes 5 minutes in clinic.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Imaging the lacrimal drainage system with contrast or a radiolabelled tracer to locate the level of obstruction before surgery
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Contrast or drops placed in the eye; X-ray, CT, or gamma-camera images taken over 10-30 minutes. Used when anatomy is unclear or primary probing has failed.

treatmentOverview: Treatment of acute dacryocystitis has three sequential phases: control the acute infection, drain any abscess, and then correct the underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction. In uncomplicated acute dacryocystitis without abscess, the first-line treatment is oral antibiotics targeted at the likely pathogens (Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus, gram-negative organisms) alongside warm compresses 3-4 times daily to encourage localization and spontaneous drainage. Co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) 625 mg three times daily for 7-10 days is the standard empirical choice in most guidelines; cefalexin is an alternative in penicillin-allergic patients without MRSA risk. Hospital admission and intravenous antibiotics (co-amoxiclav 1.2 g every 8 hours, or piperacillin-tazobactam in severe cases) are needed for: systemic sepsis, diabetic or immunocompromised patients, failure to respond to oral therapy within 48 hours, preseptal or orbital spread on CT, or neonatal dacryocystitis. Abscess drainage is performed by incision over the most fluctuant point under local anaesthesia, avoiding the medial canthal structures. Cultures are taken at drainage. Topical antibiotic drops (fluoroquinolone or tobramycin) reduce conjunctival colonisation but are adjunctive, not primary treatment. Once the acute episode has fully resolved — typically within 2-4 weeks — definitive surgical correction is required to prevent recurrence. Dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) creates a new drainage channel between the lacrimal sac and the nasal cavity, bypassing the obstructed nasolacrimal duct. Both endonasal (endoscopic) and external DCR achieve cure rates of 85-95%. Deferring surgery allows over 85% recurrence of dacryocystitis within 12-18 months.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate 625 mg three times daily, 7-10 days)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line empirical antibiotic for uncomplicated adult acute dacryocystitis. Covers Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, and gram-negative bacteria. Taken with food to minimize GI side-effects. Switch to IV if systemic features or failure to improve within 48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical resolution of uncomplicated acute dacryocystitis in 70-80% of patients with prompt oral therapy; culture-directed adjustment improves this further

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous co-amoxiclav (1.2 g every 8 hours) or piperacillin-tazobactam (4.5 g every 8 hours)
medicalTreatments.description: Used for severe dacryocystitis with systemic sepsis, orbital spread, facial cellulitis beyond the sac region, or immunosuppressed patients. Step-down to oral antibiotics once systemic signs resolve.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Controls spreading cellulitis and sepsis in 85-90% of hospitalized patients within 48-72 hours; orbital abscess may require surgical drainage

medicalTreatments.name: Warm compresses (3-4 times daily, 10-15 minutes each)
medicalTreatments.description: Accelerate pointing and spontaneous rupture of superficial abscess, improve local blood flow, and reduce pain. Combined with antibiotic therapy as the standard conservative regimen.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces time to resolution and may avoid surgical drainage in 60-70% of abscesses that do not yet require incision

medicalTreatments.name: Topical antibiotic drops (ciprofloxacin 0.3%, tobramycin 0.3%)
medicalTreatments.description: Adjunctive treatment to reduce conjunctival colonization and discharge. Do not replace systemic antibiotics as they cannot adequately penetrate the lacrimal sac.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Adjunctive reduction of discharge and conjunctival inflammation; no trial evidence that topical alone is sufficient for acute dacryocystitis

medicalTreatments.name: Incision and drainage (I&D) of lacrimal sac abscess
medicalTreatments.description: Performed under local anaesthesia through a vertical skin incision over the most fluctuant point below the medial canthus. Pus is cultured; the cavity is irrigated. A drain or wick may be placed for 24-48 hours.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Rapid decompression; pain relief within hours; reduces systemic sepsis within 24-48 hours; does not correct underlying duct obstruction

surgicalOptions.name: Dacryocystorhinostomy (external DCR)
surgicalOptions.description: The reference-standard definitive surgery for nasolacrimal duct obstruction. A bony window is made between the lacrimal sac and nasal mucosa through a skin incision adjacent to the nose, creating a permanent internal drainage fistula. Performed under general or local-sedation anaesthesia; typically 30-45 minutes.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Anatomical success (patent drainage) in 85-95% of cases; symptomatic success (resolution of epiphora and infection) in 80-90% at 5 years in prospective series

surgicalOptions.name: Endonasal (endoscopic) DCR
surgicalOptions.description: Creates the same lacrimal-nasal drainage channel but via a rigid nasal endoscope without an external skin incision. Preferred by many oculoplastic and ENT surgeons for cosmesis and when nasal disease is present. Comparable success rates to external DCR in experienced hands.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 85-95% anatomical success in contemporary endoscopic series; equivalent to external DCR for primary obstruction; slightly lower for revision or complex cases

surgicalOptions.name: Nasolacrimal duct probing and syringing (neonatal/infant)
surgicalOptions.description: Simple in-office or outpatient procedure for congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction persisting beyond 12 months: a fine metal probe is passed through the inferior canaliculus to rupture the Hasner membrane. Often performed under topical anaesthesia in cooperative infants, or under brief general anaesthesia.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Success (resolution of epiphora) in approximately 95% of cases when performed before 2 years of age; lower success rates after 3 years; bicanalicular intubation or DCR required for failures

alternativeTreatments.name: Lacrimal massage (Crigler technique)
alternativeTreatments.description: In neonates with congenital duct obstruction (not acute infection), gentle downward compression over the lacrimal sac 5-10 times twice daily creates hydrostatic pressure to open the distal membrane. Standard first-line management before probing; effective in 60-90% of cases when started in the first weeks of life.

whySeeSpecialist: Acute dacryocystitis requires ophthalmological assessment to confirm the diagnosis, assess for orbital involvement, collect cultures before antibiotics, and plan definitive surgical treatment. Recurrent or persistent dacryocystitis needs oculoplastic or lacrimal specialist evaluation for DCR; without surgery, over 85% of patients experience recurrence within 18 months. Any orbital signs or reduced vision demand same-day specialist assessment.
doctorSelectionGuide: An ophthalmologist with oculoplastic or lacrimal subspecialty training is best placed to manage both the acute infection and the definitive surgical correction. For endoscopic DCR, many centers co-operate with ENT surgeons. For neonatal cases, a pediatric ophthalmologist should assess the infant once antibiotic treatment is initiated.
hospitalCriteria: Ophthalmology emergency assessment with access to slit-lamp and lacrimal examination
CT scanner available for suspected abscess or orbital spread
Operating room equipped for DCR (external or endoscopic) after infection resolves
IV antibiotic capability and inpatient ward for severe cases
Access to ENT for combined endoscopic DCR in centers using that approach
keyFacilities: Ophthalmology emergency department or combined medical eye unit
Day surgery unit for DCR and lacrimal procedures
Pediatric ophthalmology for neonatal and infant cases
preventionStrategies: Refer persistent unilateral epiphora (watering eye) for lacrimal assessment before dacryocystitis develops — early identification and treatment of obstruction is more straightforward than managing recurrent acute infection.
Complete the prescribed course of antibiotics for the first episode and proceed to DCR consultation at 4-6 weeks to prevent recurrence.
Treat chronic sinonasal disease (rhinosinusitis, polyps) early, as nasal pathology contributes to secondary nasolacrimal duct stenosis.
Practice nasolacrimal massage (Crigler technique) as instructed in neonates with congenital duct obstruction to reduce the chance of secondary infection.
Attend follow-up ophthalmology appointments after midfacial trauma and sinus surgery to detect post-traumatic duct stenosis before it progresses to recurrent infection.
lifestyleModifications: Complete the full antibiotic course, even if symptoms improve within 24-48 hours, to avoid incomplete treatment and abscess or orbital spread.
Apply warm compresses three times daily during the acute phase to encourage localization and ease pain.
Avoid squeezing or repeatedly compressing the lacrimal sac vigorously once infection is established, as this risks bacteremia; gentle circular massage is acceptable.
Keep the eye area clean by gently removing discharge with clean cotton wool dampened with cooled boiled water.
Proceed to DCR surgery after the acute episode resolves; do not defer indefinitely — recurrence is almost certain without correction of the underlying obstruction.

recommended: Adequate protein and vitamin C intake to support wound healing and immune response during acute infection
Well-hydrated state to support systemic antibiotic distribution and immune function
Iron-rich foods to correct any anemia in patients with chronic recurring infections
Balanced diet with no specific restrictions for uncomplicated dacryocystitis
avoid: Alcohol during antibiotic courses — interacts with metronidazole if prescribed; impairs immune response
Smoking — impairs wound healing and mucociliary clearance, potentially worsening nasal contributing factors
Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) without guidance in patients with penicillin allergy, as they may mask fever without treating infection
Contact lenses during acute infection as they increase risk of bacterial conjunctivitis and corneal involvement

exerciseGuidelines: Bed rest is not required for uncomplicated acute dacryocystitis; normal activity is usually possible once antibiotics are started and fever subsides. Avoid vigorous facial exercise or activities that cause facial flushing during active infection. After DCR surgery, avoid heavy lifting, nose-blowing, and water sports for 2-4 weeks as advised by the operating surgeon.
dailyManagement: Take prescribed antibiotics at evenly spaced intervals throughout the day; do not skip doses in the first 48 hours.
Apply warm compresses 10-15 minutes three times daily to the medial canthus region.
Keep discharge gently cleaned from the eye using clean cotton pads; do not share face cloths.
Monitor for warning signs of spreading infection or orbital involvement: increased proptosis, reduced eye movement, or vision change — return same day.
Instil prescribed antibiotic drops as directed to reduce conjunctival colonization.
Attend outpatient review within 48-72 hours of starting antibiotics; non-response requires IV escalation or abscess drainage.
prognosis: Acute dacryocystitis responds well to prompt systemic antibiotics, with clinical resolution of the acute episode in 70-85% of uncomplicated cases within 5-7 days of treatment. Lacrimal sac abscess complicates approximately 15-20% of episodes and requires incision and drainage, but outcomes after drainage and antibiotics are still good. Without definitive surgical correction, recurrence occurs in over 85% of adult patients within 12-18 months, as the underlying nasolacrimal duct obstruction persists. Dacryocystorhinostomy achieves permanent cure (resolution of both recurrent infection and epiphora) in 85-95% of patients in contemporary series. The main risk of permanent sequelae is orbital spread of infection — orbital abscess and orbital cellulitis occur in fewer than 5% of cases but can cause diplopia, permanent vision loss, or intracranial extension if not treated emergently. Neonatal dacryocystitis treated promptly has an excellent prognosis; most cases resolve completely with antibiotics and massage, with probing needed in less than 10% of infants with congenital obstruction.
recoveryTimeline: Fever and acute pain improve within 24-48 hours of starting antibiotics. Swelling and erythema resolve over 5-10 days. Epiphora and discharge persist until the obstruction is treated surgically. After DCR surgery, patients are symptom-free in 4-8 weeks; external DCR leaves a faint scar that fades over 3-6 months.
complications: Lacrimal sac abscess — in 15-20% of acute episodes, the sac progresses to frank pus-filled cavity requiring incision and drainage in addition to antibiotics.
Preseptal cellulitis — extension of infection to the eyelid and cheek requires IV antibiotics; distinguished from orbital cellulitis by preserved eye movements and normal vision.

Orbital cellulitis and subperiosteal or orbital abscess — rare but serious spread through the orbital septum causing proptosis, ophthalmoplegia, and risk of vision loss; requires urgent CT and surgical drainage.
Lacrimal sac fistula — spontaneous rupture of an untreated abscess creates a permanent sinus tract to the skin surface; requires formal surgical closure.
Dacryocystocele (mucocele) — in neonates with congenital obstruction, failure to drain produces a cystic enlargement of the sac compressing the nasal airway; requires urgent decompression.
Chronic epiphora and recurrent dacryocystitis — persisting without surgical correction, the obstruction causes permanent watering and a recurrence rate above 85% within 18 months.

supportResources.name: American Academy of Ophthalmology — Dacryocystitis
supportResources.description: AAO patient education page covering symptoms, diagnosis, and surgical treatment of dacryocystitis.

supportResources.name: AAPOS (American Association for Pediatric Ophthalmology and Strabismus)
supportResources.description: Guidance on congenital nasolacrimal duct obstruction and neonatal dacryocystitis management for parents.

supportResources.name: BOPSS (British Oculoplastic Surgery Society)
supportResources.description: Patient information on lacrimal disease and DCR surgery from the UK oculoplastic surgery professional body.

supportResources.name: NHS Patient Information — Watering Eye and DCR
supportResources.description: NHS guidance on watering eyes, causes, and referral for surgical treatment including DCR.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute conjunctivitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute conjunctivitis presents with diffuse redness of the conjunctiva, sticky discharge, and itching without focal swelling or tenderness at the medial canthus. Dacryocystitis produces a distinct painful swelling below and medial to the inner canthus with epiphora. Regurgitation of pus on lacrimal sac pressure is pathognomonic of dacryocystitis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute iridocyclitis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Iridocyclitis causes deep eye pain, photophobia, and circumcorneal redness but no external facial swelling or lacrimal sac tenderness. It is an intraocular diagnosis confirmed on slit-lamp. Dacryocystitis is an external periocular infection confirmed by palpation and CT, without intraocular inflammation.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute angle-closure glaucoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Acute angle closure causes severe eye pain with diffuse ocular redness, high IOP, corneal haze, a mid-dilated pupil, and nausea — but no focal periorbital swelling or medial canthal tenderness. Dacryocystitis has normal IOP, clear cornea, and a localized swelling at the lacrimal sac region.

relatedConditions.name: Acute iridocyclitis
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are acute ophthalmologic inflammations causing periocular pain and redness; their distinct anatomical locations and presentations require careful clinical differentiation.

relatedConditions.name: Acquired color vision deficiency
relatedConditions.relevance: Both are acquired ophthalmological conditions managed by ophthalmologists; dacryocystitis with orbital spread can in rare cases impair visual function.

relatedConditions.name: Glaucoma
relatedConditions.relevance: Glaucoma drops (latanoprost, bimatoprost) are occasionally associated with canalicular obstruction and secondary dacryocystitis, particularly with long-term use.

faqs.question: What is acute dacryocystitis?
faqs.answer: Acute dacryocystitis is a bacterial infection of the lacrimal sac — the small tear-collecting pouch at the inner corner of the eye. It results from blockage of the nasolacrimal duct, which allows tears to stagnate and become infected. It presents as a sudden, painful, red swelling at the inner corner of the eye below the medial canthus.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of dacryocystitis?
faqs.answer: The main symptoms are acute pain, redness, and swelling at the medial corner of the eye, specifically below the inner corner fold. The eye waters constantly (epiphora), and pressing gently on the swelling often expresses pus or mucus through the punctum. Fever may accompany severe episodes.

faqs.question: What causes acute dacryocystitis?
faqs.answer: The cause is almost always blockage (stenosis) of the nasolacrimal duct, which allows tears to stagnate in the lacrimal sac and become colonized by bacteria — typically Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus, or Haemophilus. In adults, the blockage is usually from involutional scarring. In newborns it results from a membrane at the lower duct end that failed to open at birth.

faqs.question: How is acute dacryocystitis treated?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated acute dacryocystitis is treated with oral co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) for 7-10 days alongside warm compresses applied 3-4 times daily. If an abscess forms, it requires surgical incision and drainage. Patients with systemic sepsis need IV antibiotics in hospital. Definitive cure requires DCR surgery after the acute episode resolves.

faqs.question: Will dacryocystitis go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Mild episodes may partially settle, but without antibiotics and eventual surgical correction of the duct blockage, dacryocystitis almost always recurs. Over 85% of patients experience another attack within 12-18 months without DCR surgery. Untreated infection can progress to abscess or orbital cellulitis.

faqs.question: What is DCR surgery and is it curative?
faqs.answer: Dacryocystorhinostomy (DCR) creates a permanent new drainage channel from the lacrimal sac directly into the nasal cavity, bypassing the blocked nasolacrimal duct. External and endoscopic techniques both achieve cure rates of 85-95%. It is the definitive treatment for nasolacrimal duct obstruction and prevents recurrent dacryocystitis.

faqs.question: Is dacryocystitis serious?
faqs.answer: Uncomplicated dacryocystitis responds well to antibiotics. However, it can be serious if it progresses to a lacrimal sac abscess (15-20% of cases), spreads to the orbit as orbital cellulitis, or becomes septic. Orbital spread causes proptosis, eye movement restriction, and vision threat and requires urgent CT and IV antibiotics or surgery.

faqs.question: Can a baby get dacryocystitis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Neonatal dacryocystitis occurs in the first weeks of life when a congenital membrane at the lower end of the nasolacrimal duct (Hasner's valve) fails to open at birth. It presents with a red swollen medial canthus, watering, and discharge. Treated with topical antibiotics, lacrimal massage, and systemic antibiotics if febrile; probing if the blockage persists beyond 12-13 months.

faqs.question: How do I know if it is dacryocystitis or a stye?
faqs.answer: A stye (hordeolum) is on the eyelid margin, centered on an eyelash follicle, and causes a tender lump on or near the lid edge without epiphora. Dacryocystitis causes a distinct swelling below and medial to the inner canthus (not on the lid), with watering and pus expressible from the tear duct. The locations are clearly different.

faqs.question: Why is dacryocystitis more common in women?
faqs.answer: Women have an anatomically narrower bony nasolacrimal canal than men, making duct stenosis more likely with age-related involution. Hormonal changes after menopause may also contribute to mucosal atrophy in the duct. This explains why women account for 55-75% of adult dacryocystitis cases in most ophthalmology series.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for dacryocystitis?
faqs.answer: First-line oral antibiotic is co-amoxiclav (amoxicillin-clavulanate) 625 mg three times daily for 7-10 days, covering Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and common gram-negative bacteria. Cefalexin is an alternative for penicillin-allergic patients. IV co-amoxiclav or piperacillin-tazobactam is used for severe cases requiring hospital admission.

faqs.question: What happens if dacryocystitis is not treated?
faqs.answer: Untreated dacryocystitis progresses to lacrimal sac abscess in 15-20% of cases and can spread to cause preseptal or orbital cellulitis, threatening vision. A spontaneous fistula to the skin surface may develop. Without surgical correction, recurrent episodes are almost inevitable.

faqs.question: Does dacryocystitis recur after antibiotics?
faqs.answer: Yes. Antibiotics treat the acute infection but do not correct the underlying nasolacrimal duct blockage. Without DCR surgery, recurrence occurs in over 85% of adult patients within 12-18 months. Following the initial episode with elective DCR surgery is the standard of care to prevent recurrence.

faqs.question: Is DCR surgery painful?
faqs.answer: DCR is performed under general or local-sedation anaesthesia; patients feel no pain during the procedure. Postoperative discomfort is mild and managed with paracetamol or ibuprofen. External DCR leaves a small scar at the side of the nose that fades significantly over 3-6 months; endoscopic DCR leaves no external scar.

faqs.question: Can I use eye drops instead of surgery?
faqs.answer: Eye drops treat conjunctival colonization and discharge but cannot open a blocked nasolacrimal duct or cure dacryocystitis. Antibiotics (oral or IV) control acute infection. Surgery (DCR) is required for definitive cure. Long-term topical antibiotics without surgery are not a recommended management strategy.

simpleName: Acute dacryocystitis (infected tear sac, lacrimal sac infection)
metaDescription: Acute dacryocystitis: infected lacrimal sac causing painful inner-eye swelling. Antibiotic treatment, abscess drainage and DCR surgery reviewed.
